(r)
 ontents
 he ospel o f ruth   is a hristian sermon on the theme of salvation by acquaintance
with god   (gnosis).   ne of the most brilliantly crafted works of ancient hristian
literature, in the original reek it must have had a rhetorical power that ranked with
the great masterpieces   of hristian prose.   t is the earliest surviving sermon   of
hristian mysticism. nd since very few sermons survive from the ancient gnostic
sect and its offshoots, r affords a rare glimpse of the actual human atmosphere
of a church meeting, in which a magisterial gnostic preacher addresses a congregation,
speaking from personal authority. he main themes of the work are established in
the opening sentence search for the father (god) and the hope of deliverance on the
part of those who had fallen ignorant and needed a savior to ransom them from
ignorance and emission of the saving divine ord, who proclaimed the truth about
the father and brought joy and acquaintance with the father. he contents are thus
a dynamic description of hristian   gnosis   or acquaintance with god.
he characters of this theological drama are simply the   fa ther   (the unknowable
god),   the   ord   or   son   (god’s   manifestation),   and   the   ignorant,   who   become
transformed into   those who have acquaintance.   he work is overtly hristian, and
makes no specific reference to the gnostic myth. t speaks of esus’ crucifiion as
the central object of hristian faith, and contains many paraphrases of ew estament
passages.
wo possible states of being are contrasted in r repose (true being, wakefulness)
and movement (illusory eistence, nightmare).  similar distinction is observed in
latonism, but the basic world view of r is not simply latonist. ather, the
cosmological   model   of   r   is   provided   by   toic   pantheistic   monism   and   by
astronomy. od (the father) is held to be uncontained and to contain all things.
ndividuals within him are also said to contain god thus god permeates, or can
permeate, all individual things. ccordingly, the model is not linear (as in gnostic
mysticism, cf. s, r) but three-dimensional and nested, like the ancient astronomical
concept of nested heavenly spheres, with god as the most encompassing sphere.
et unlike toic cosmology, the system of r is strongly antimaterialist, even
illusionist,   as   regards   the   reality   of   material   structures.   ne   consequence   of
acquaintance ( gnosis ) with the all-containing divine father is to see the illusion that
there are material things—indeed the illusion of distinction and structure—fade away
into nothingness. his amounts to reunion with the father it is salvation, and repose.
he main register of r is thus not description of the universe but discussion of
knowledge and psychology. n this, the concept of salvation in r closely resembles
that   of   he   ospel ccording   to   homas.   n   the   few   passages   where   mythic
cosmology may covertly come into view (e.g. f) the figures and events of myth
are psychological. n this sense, r is to cosmological myth (r) as allegory (cf.

the “ istorical ntroduction”   to art hree) is to tet.   n this almost complete
allegorization, the underlying dynamic of gnostic myth (fullness—lack—recapture
of the lacked) is reapplied microcosmically, at the level of the individual hristian.
he theology of r uses the simple biblical language of “ father” and “ son” (or
possibly “ parent” and “ offspring,” though   l f seems to apply a specifically male
anatomical metaphor to the parent). t has been demonstrated that in r alentinus
paraphrases, and so interprets, some thirty to sity scriptural passages, almost all
from ew estament books (n, n,  n, v, t, m,  o,  o, p, ol, and
eb). f these, it has been shown that the ohannine literature (including v) has
had the most profound theological influence upon alentinus’s thought the auline
literature, less so and t hardly at all. o a large degree the paraphrased passages
have been verbally reshaped by abridgement or substitution, to make them agree
with alentinus’s own theological perspective (cf. the paraphrase of n in ).
hough carefully controlled, the rhetoric of r is' not linear but atmospheric,
just as its cosmology is not linear but concentric r aims not to argue a thesis by
logic, but to describe, evoke, and elicit a kind of relationship. deas and images are
developed slowly by repeating key points with minor changes. s in gnostic myth
a great   many   epithets   used   substantively   are   applied   to   each   main   character.
mbiguity of the pronouns “ he” and “ it” plays a major role in this development
this is one of the striking aspects of alentinus’s style, and can be seen also in the
ragments. alentinus’s style—quite apart from his mystic theology or theory of
salvation—is probably unique within ancient hristian literature it has been described
as a gnostic rhetoric.
                         
 iterary background
 he manuscripts do not specify the title or author of r. he conventional title
has been supplied by scholarship it may be a mistake to suppose that alentinus
ever gave a title to the work. n any case, the second-century father of the church,
t.   renaeus   of   yon,   states   that   the   alentinian   church   read   a   ospel   (or
 roclamation) o f ruth.   ince this is the opening phrase of r, some scholars have
concluded that renaeus must be referring to the present work.
he author’s name does not appear in the manuscripts, and thus the attribution
of r to alentinus remains hypothetical. evertheless, it is etremely likely for
several reasons the work’s stylistic resemblance to the ragments (whose attribution
is eplicit) and the uniqueness of that style the alleged genius and eloquence of
alentinus and the lack of a likely candidate for the authorship among later alentinian
writers and the absence of a developed system in the work, perhaps suggesting that
it belongs early in the history of the alentinian church.
he place and eact date of composition of r are unknown (alentinus died
ca. ) the language of composition was reek.
he work is a sermon and has nothing to do with the hristian genre properly
called “ gospel” (e.g. the ospel of ark).
 et
 he original reek apparently does not survive, though a remark by t. renaeus
(see above, “ iterary background” ) may be taken as testimony to its eistence.
he tet is known only in optic translation, attested by two manuscripts, j 
(-) and   (fragments), which were copied just before   a   . d   .    and are
now in the airo optic useum. he two optic manuscripts contain different
versions of the tet, one ( ) in a ubachmimic dialect of optic and the other
( ) in the ahidic dialect of optic. he two versions seem to have been
translated from slightly different ancient editions of the reek tet. he ahidic
manuscript ( ) has been almost completely destroyed and survives in the

form of a few fragments the ubachmimic manuscript ( ) is virtually complete.
or that reason, the present translation is from the ubachmimic  ( ) alone.
he translation below is based upon the critical edition of the optic by alinine
et   al.,   with   some   alterations   and   with   improved   readings   introduced   from   an
unpublished collation of the manuscript made by . mmel and kindly supplied by

 isappearance of the material world
    or where there is envy and strife there is a lack, but where unity is,
    there is completion. «nasmuch as the lack came into being because the
father was not known, from the moment that the father is known the
     lack will not eist. «s with one person’s ignorance (of another)—when
one becomes acquainted, ignorance of the other passes away of its own

i    so also lack passes away in completion, and so from that moment
on, the realm of appearance is no longer manifest but rather will pass
away in the harmony of unity.
  .   or now their affairs are dispersed. «ut when unity makes the ways
complete,   it is in unity   that all will gather themselves,   and   it is by
acquaintance that all will purify themselves out of multiplicity into unity,
consuming matter within themselves as fire, and darkness by light, and    o    
     death by life. «o since these things have happened to each of us, it is
    fitting for us to meditate upon the entirety, «so that this house might be
holy and quietly intent on unity.
  parable of jars
   ,    t is like some people who moved to a new house. «hey had some
jars that in places were no good, and these got broken but the owner
of the house suffered no loss, rather the owner was glad because instead
of the bad jars it was (now) the full ones that they would be going to
     use up. «or this is the judgment that has come    from above, having   jn   i
judged everyone—a drawn two-edged sword cutting this way and that,   ebi
since the ord that is in the heart of those that speak it, has come   jn ii
. forward. «t is not just a sound, but it became a body. « great disturbance
has come to pass among the jars for some have leaked dry, some are
half full, some are well filled, some have been spilled, some have been
washed, and still others broken.
                            
  a.   “ lack" in gnostic myth, the missing   wisdom. n r the lack is mutual, between
power stolen by aldabaoth from its mother   the divine fullness and the individual aeon.

ament and downfall of error

 ,   nor stability «and error became ecited, not knowing what to do •she]

they saluted the father in truth and power (so) perfect that it set them

 otential being and real being
 io   .   i i   ll the waysb   are his emanations. «hey know that they have emanated
from him like children who were within a mature man but knew they
is had not yet received form nor been given name. «t is when they receive
the impulse toward acquaintance with the father that he gives birth to

    him. «he father himself is perfect and acquainted with every way that
    is in him. «f he wills, what he wills appears, as he gives it form and

eistence.
    hose who have not yet come to be are not acquainted with the one
    who put them in order. «ow,  am not saying that those who have not
 yet come to be are nothing «rather, that they eist    within him who
might will that they come to be, if he wills at some future time, as it
    were. «efore all things have appeared he is personally acquainted with
